narrative_blocks.header: Edwards recorded the exact hour of her birth
narrative_blocks.body: She was born August 31, 1736 in Northampton — 'between 2 and 3 o'clock in the morning,' as her father Jonathan Edwards recorded in the family Bible. She was a Northampton child of the Great Awakening years.

narrative_blocks.header: Married Jahleel Woodbridge of Stockbridge in 1764
narrative_blocks.body: In 1764 she married Jahleel Woodbridge — Princeton 1761, judge of Probate Court, Connecticut state senator, captain of Minute Men. He served in the Provincial Congress with her brother Timothy Edwards.

narrative_blocks.header: Stockbridge home base for the Edwards-Woodbridge line
narrative_blocks.body: She moved to Stockbridge with Jahleel Woodbridge — the same Mohican mission town where her father had ministered after Northampton. She died in Stockbridge in 1786, age fifty.

narrative_blocks.header: Edwards recorded the exact hour of her birth
narrative_blocks.body: She was born August 31, 1736 in Northampton — 'between 2 and 3 o'clock in the morning,' as her father Jonathan Edwards recorded in the family Bible. She was a Northampton child of the Great Awakening years.

narrative_blocks.header: Married Jahleel Woodbridge of Stockbridge in 1764
narrative_blocks.body: In 1764 she married Jahleel Woodbridge — Princeton 1761, judge of Probate Court, Connecticut state senator, captain of Minute Men. He served in the Provincial Congress with her brother Timothy Edwards.

narrative_blocks.header: Stockbridge home base for the Edwards-Woodbridge line
narrative_blocks.body: She moved to Stockbridge with Jahleel Woodbridge — the same Mohican mission town where her father had ministered after Northampton. She died in Stockbridge in 1786, age fifty.

narrative_blocks.header: Kidnapped from home at midnight by Shays men
narrative_blocks.body: When Shays' Rebellion reached Stockbridge in 1787, the insurrectionists targeted Judge Woodbridge — symbol of the 'silk stocking aristocracy.' They broke into his home at midnight, pillaged it, and carried him off as a prisoner to Great Barrington; he negotiated his own release and met the rescue party halfway home. Edward Bellamy's 1879 novel 'The Duke of Stockbridge' drew on these events.

narrative_blocks.header: Learned the Mahican language at Mission School
narrative_blocks.body: Growing up in Stockbridge from age one, he attended the Indian Mission School and was tutored by his uncle Timothy Woodbridge and Jonathan Edwards — learning the Mahican language in the process. Princeton followed in 1761, and Berkshire County militia service by 1764.

narrative_blocks.header: Paid Stockbridge Indian soldiers to prevent strong drink
narrative_blocks.body: On 5 July 1775 the Third Provincial Congress of Massachusetts resolved that wages of Stockbridge Indians serving in the war be paid directly to Woodbridge and Timothy Edwards. The Indians had requested the arrangement themselves — 'being sensible of their want of prudence in disposing of their money, [they were] desirous that this Congress, in their wisdom, would devise some method to prevent their getting too much strong drink.'

orbit_connections.name: William Cullen Bryant
orbit_connections.relationship: Read law 1811-1814 in the Jonathan Woodbridge House, Worthington MA
orbit_connections.notes: William Cullen Bryant (1794-1878) — first American poet of international standing; editor of the New York Evening Post. "Thanatopsis" believed written in part at the Woodbridge House 1811-1813. Later established Central Park, the Metropolitan Museum of Art, the Century Association; patron of the Hudson River School of painters. (Worthington Historical Society; landvest.blog 2013)

institution_affiliations.notes: West Point graduate; served as 2nd Lieutenant US Army 1826-1829.

narrative_blocks.header: Nine hours too late for Jeb Stuart
narrative_blocks.detail: Woodbridge had served as 2nd Lieutenant in the US Army (1826-1829) before entering the Episcopal priesthood. By 1864 he had been Rector of the Monumental Church in Richmond for 21 years — one of the most prominent Episcopal congregations in the Confederacy. His willingness to make the dangerous overnight journey for Mrs. Stuart speaks to both his personal courage and his central place in Richmond's wartime religious community. (Historical accounts; FindAGrave; Wikipedia on Flora Stuart)
narrative_blocks.body: On May 12, 1864, the day after General Jeb Stuart was mortally wounded at Yellow Tavern, Rev. Woodbridge escorted Flora Cooke Stuart on an overnight journey from Beaverdam, Virginia, to Richmond to reach her husband. The journey — by train to Ashland, then fifteen miles by ambulance wagon through rain and military detours — took well over nine hours. Stuart died before Flora could reach him.

narrative_blocks.header: Thirty-five years in Richmond’s pulpit
narrative_blocks.body: The West Point lieutenant turned priest held Monumental Church from 1843 until his death in 1878 — through secession, the war, and the fall of the city around him. On the Flora Stuart night a nervous Confederate sentinel nearly shot him.

narrative_blocks.header: Marriage united Edwards biographer and missionary families
narrative_blocks.body: Joseph Woodbridge's 1798 marriage to Louisa Hopkins united two important New England religious lineages in the town his own family had founded. Louisa's father Colonel Mark Hopkins was the younger brother of Rev. Samuel Hopkins D.D. — the first biographer of Jonathan Edwards and Edwards's student in his home. Her mother Electa Sergeant was the daughter of Rev. John Sergeant, the first missionary to the Indian Mission at Stockbridge Massachusetts.

narrative_blocks.header: Wife's grandfather founded Stockbridge Indian Mission
narrative_blocks.body: Louisa Hopkins's grandfather Rev. John Sergeant A.M. had been the founding missionary to the Stockbridge Indians in the 1730s — establishing the mission that gave the town its identity and made it nationally famous as a site of early American Protestant missionary work. The Woodbridge-Sergeant-Hopkins triangle clustered three of New England's significant religious-historical surnames in a single Stockbridge household in the 1810s.

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter Catherine married into Pitkin family line
narrative_blocks.body: Joseph and Louisa's eldest daughter Catherine Louisa Woodbridge (1801-1863) married Timothy Pitkin — connecting the Woodbridge line to the wider Pitkin family, which included the Connecticut Congressman Timothy Pitkin (1766-1847), a notable politician of the same surname though distinct branch. Catherine and Timothy's daughter Katharine Louise Pitkin married Robert Henry James, opening the Frederick Pitkin James line for the next generation.

narrative_blocks.header: Born and died Stockbridge MA, age fifty-seven
narrative_blocks.body: Joseph Woodbridge was born July 22, 1771 in Stockbridge Massachusetts and died there April 23, 1829 at age fifty-seven. He is buried at Stockbridge Cemetery. He had three children with Louisa Hopkins: Catherine Louisa (1801-1863), Joseph Effingham (1806-1881), and William (1814-1837 died young at twenty-three).

orbit_connections.name: Gov. Frederick Walker Pitkin (1837-1886)
orbit_connections.relationship: 2nd Governor of Colorado; probable Connecticut Pitkin cousin of HD2206's husband Timothy Pitkin (1792-1874)
orbit_connections.notes: Frederick Walker Pitkin (Aug 31, 1837 – Dec 18, 1886) was the 2nd Governor of Colorado 1879-1883. Born in Manchester, Hartford County CT — the same county as Timothy Pitkin the merchant (East Hartford CT, X00705, husband of HD2206). Frederick's father was Eli Pitkin (c.1799-1849, Manchester CT), son of Eleazer Strong Pitkin (1763-1822). Timothy (1792) and Eli (c.1799) were almost certainly first cousins in the Connecticut Pitkin family — same county, same generation. NAMED FOR: Pitkin County Colorado, Pitkin Avenue in multiple Colorado cities and towns (Grand Junction, Glenwood Springs, Pueblo, Saguache), and the town of Pitkin, Colorado. Governor Pitkin married Fidelia James of Lockport NY — NOTE: This is a separate James family from Robert Henry James (X00707, husband of HD2235 Katharine Louise Pitkin James); the shared surname is coincidental. Gov. Pitkin is NOT a Hooker descendant — his line does not pass through Catherine Louisa Woodbridge. Worth further research to confirm the Timothy/Eli cousinship. (Wikipedia; FindAGrave)

institution_affiliations.notes: Graduated Jefferson Medical College Philadelphia 1833. Returned to Youngstown; served as Civil War surgeon at Johnson's Island; appointed by President Hayes to Fort Peck Montana 1879.

narrative_blocks.header: First doctor born in Youngstown
narrative_blocks.detail: He barely survived the Mahoning River drowning that killed his brother John. Jefferson Medical College 1833 — the leading medical school in the US at that time. In 1847-48, David Tod (later Ohio Governor) personally chose him as family physician for his diplomatic posting in Brazil. In 1861, he was appointed surgeon at Johnson's Island on Lake Erie — the Union's prisoner-of-war camp for Confederate officers. In 1879, President Hayes appointed him surgeon at Fort Peck, Montana, where he was the medical officer for both soldiers and Native Americans. He co-founded the Mahoning County Medical Society (1872) and served as its first president for seven years. His billing eccentricities — listing "the fat woman in Brier Hill" in his day books and accepting half-fees from the poor — were celebrated in the 1882 History of Trumbull and Mahoning Counties. (Rose Melnick Medical Museum; Growing Up in Working Class Youngstown; US Army records)
narrative_blocks.body: Timothy Woodbridge was Youngstown's first native-born physician, whose career spanned from private practice in a log cabin to two separate US Army appointments — one by Lincoln's government during the Civil War, one by President Hayes in 1879.

narrative_blocks.header: “The fat woman in Brier Hill”
narrative_blocks.body: His ledgers listed patients by description rather than name — “the fat woman in Brier Hill” — and he routinely accepted half to three-quarters of what the poor owed him. The 1816 log cabin he moved across Youngstown in 1862 still stands.

career.organization: US Army, Johnson’s Island POW camp

narrative_blocks.header: Went blind at seventeen; still became a minister
narrative_blocks.body: He enrolled at Williams College at fifteen but became totally blind at seventeen — an affliction lasting until his death. He studied law by having classmates read to him, then abandoned law for theology and was ordained in 1811.

narrative_blocks.header: His nephew Mark Hopkins read for him
narrative_blocks.body: While pastoring at Green River and Spencertown, he needed an amanuensis to read and write for him. One of those who served in that role was his nephew Mark Hopkins — later the famous president of Williams College.

narrative_blocks.header: Founded two 'famous' academies in Hudson Valley
narrative_blocks.body: He founded Green River Academy in 1818, described as 'a famous school of the day.' In 1846 he founded Spencertown Academy five miles distant — the same year Williams College awarded his Doctor of Divinity.

parents.mother_research_notes: Esther Stoddard — daughter of Rev. Solomon Stoddard of Northampton
parents.father_research_notes: Rev. Timothy Edwards of East Windsor CT — minister 60 years until death 1758 age 89

church_roles.notes: Succeeded grandfather Solomon Stoddard. Led Great Awakening revivals. Dismissed 1750 over communion controversy.

X00128.prominent_name_reason: Rev. Jonathan Edwards (1703-1758) — Yale 1720; greatest American theologian; leader of the Great Awakening; delivered "Sinners in the Hands of an Angry God" (1741); President of Princeton; grandfather of Aaron Burr Jr. (3rd U.S. Vice President). Father's father-in-law was Rev. Timothy Edwards who held one enslaved person named Ansars. Edwards himself enslaved at least six people: Venus (purchased 1731), Joab, Rose Binney, Titus (son of Joab and Rose), Joseph, Sue, and possibly Leah.

enslaved_persons.name: Venus
enslaved_persons.description: Purchased June 1731 as a young teenager. Likely later baptized and given name Leah.

enslaved_persons.name: Titus
enslaved_persons.description: Enslaved man. Son of Joab and Rose.

enslaved_persons.name: Joseph
enslaved_persons.description: Enslaved man. Married couple with Sue.

cross_connections.display_label: Giles Hooker Cowles studied theology under him after graduating Yale in 1789.
Succeeded his son-in-law as Princeton president after his sudden death.
Hosted him in final illness; published his Life as bestseller
Physician-minister and intimate friend in his New York years

education.notes: Entered 1716 at age 13. Graduated with highest honors.

narrative_blocks.header: His 1741 sermon shocked New England
narrative_blocks.body: On July 8, 1741, he delivered 'Sinners in the Hands of an Angry God' to a Connecticut congregation. The Great Awakening had been spreading from his Northampton parish for six years.

narrative_blocks.header: Wrote his greatest works in frontier exile
narrative_blocks.body: After Northampton he took the Stockbridge mission preaching to Housatonnuck families through an interpreter. There he composed Freedom of the Will, The Nature of True Virtue, and The Doctrine of Original Sin.

narrative_blocks.header: Town turned on him over a midwifery book
narrative_blocks.body: In 1748 he disciplined young people in his Northampton parish for circulating what he called 'immoral books,' later identified as a midwifery manual. His own Williams and Hawley relatives led the council that dismissed him in 1750.

narrative_blocks.header: Died thirty-five days into the Princeton presidency
narrative_blocks.body: He accepted the College of New Jersey presidency in early 1758 after his son-in-law Aaron Burr Sr. died suddenly in office. A smallpox inoculation administered February 23 turned fatal on March 22.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale valedictorian at sixteen, in Latin
narrative_blocks.body: He entered Yale at twelve and graduated four years later as the youngest member of his class. He delivered the commencement valedictory address in Latin.

sources.short: Life of Rev. Jonathan Edwards by Sereno Dwight

burial.plot_notes: Princeton Cemetery, Princeton, New Jersey — buried next to Jonathan Edwards

cross_connections.display_label: Died trying to bring him home from Princeton at age two.
Died trying to bring her home from Princeton at age four.
Her North Haven nephew — wealthiest man in town in 1786

bio.photo_notes: Sarah Pierpont Edwards (1710-1758), wife of Jonathan Edwards.
bio.bio_blurb: Subject of Edwards’s famous meditation at thirteen

narrative_blocks.header: Edwards wrote about her when she was twelve
narrative_blocks.body: Jonathan Edwards composed a private spiritual meditation about a 'young lady in New Haven who is beloved of that Great Being who made and rules the world.' She married him five years later, at seventeen.

narrative_blocks.header: Brought home her orphaned Burr grandchildren
narrative_blocks.body: After Jonathan's death she traveled south in autumn 1758 to retrieve four-year-old Sarah Burr and two-year-old Aaron Burr Jr. She fell sick with dysentery in Philadelphia before she could complete the journey.

narrative_blocks.header: Died exactly six months after her husband
narrative_blocks.body: Jonathan died of smallpox-inoculation complications on March 22, 1758, weeks into the Princeton presidency. Sarah died of dysentery on October 2, 1758, at forty-eight.

narrative_blocks.header: Made her will the day she died
narrative_blocks.body: On October 2, 1758, in Philadelphia, she dictated her last instructions hours before dysentery took her. The two Burr grandchildren she had been escorting were placed with Dr. William Shippen.

narrative_blocks.header: Her portrait hangs in a Yale dining hall
narrative_blocks.body: She and her husband were both painted in oils, traditionally attributed to John Smybert. The portraits were bequeathed to Yale in 1939 and now hang in Jonathan Edwards College.

cross_connections.display_label: Both founding trustees of Yale 1701; Pierson was first rector.
Pierpont built the institution Davenport had envisioned.
Descendant co-founded Skull and Bones at his Yale 130 years later.
coined the Ivy League name for colleges like Yale
his brother-in-law, a fellow Yale founder married to a Hooker sister

institutions.institution_blurb: Attended Roxbury Latin School before Harvard.

artworks.artwork_blurb: Portrait 1711 by Pierpont Limner; Yale University Art Gallery.

narrative_blocks.header: Ten ministers, forty books, one college
narrative_blocks.body: In 1700 Pierpont gathered ten ministers at Branford, each laying down volumes with the words “I give these books for the founding of a College in this Colony” — tradition credits James with six of the original forty. The charter he secured the next year became Yale.

narrative_blocks.header: Two elms planted at the minister’s door
narrative_blocks.body: A poor farmer with nothing else to give carried two young elms on his shoulder and planted them before the new parsonage in 1686. Under their shade Jonathan Edwards courted Sarah Pierpont forty years on, and Whitefield preached at sixty. When the last elm finally came down, its sound trunk was sawed into blocks and scattered among the family.

narrative_blocks.header: Three brides in seven years
narrative_blocks.body: James buried two young wives in five years — Abigail, dead of a cold caught attending church in her wedding garments per the custom of the day, and Sarah, lost after childbirth. In 1698 he married Mary Hooker of Farmington, who bore eight children and outlived him by twenty-six years.

narrative_blocks.header: He drafted Connecticut’s church constitution
narrative_blocks.body: A leader of the 1708 Saybrook Synod, Pierpont is said to have drawn up the Articles of the Saybrook Platform — the system of discipline and fellowship that governed Connecticut’s churches for more than a century.

narrative_blocks.header: 1706 ordination — five ministers, all Hooker-tree
narrative_blocks.body: On November 25, 1706 he was among five ministers who ordained Rev. John Hart at East Guilford — alongside Buckingham of Saybrook, Noadiah Russell of Middletown, Samuel Russell of Branford, and Thomas Ruggles of Guilford. All five names run through the Hooker tree.

narrative_blocks.header: The household that seeded three centuries
narrative_blocks.body: The New Haven parsonage became one of America’s great seedbeds: daughter Sarah married Jonathan Edwards, and the lines that followed carried two Timothy Dwights and Theodore Dwight Woolsey to Yale’s presidency, a vice president named Burr — and, further on, a financier named John Pierpont Morgan.

education.notes: Oldest boys’ school in the colonies, founded 1645.

career.notes: Ordained July 2 1685, ending an eleven-year pulpit vacancy; served until death.
The school became Yale College in 1718.

cross_connections.display_label: fathered the New Haven minister she married in 1698
was mother to the New Haven minister she married in 1698
her granddaughter, last of the line the heirloom apron passed through

notable.notable_blurb: Wife of Yale co-founder Rev. James Pierpont

artworks.artwork_blurb: Companion portrait to husband Rev. James Pierpont, 1711; Yale Art Gallery.

narrative_blocks.header: Eight children in the house under the elms
narrative_blocks.body: Mary ran the New Haven parsonage through sixteen years of marriage and eight children, then presided over it as a widow for twenty-six more. The congregation had raised the house by voluntary subscription, and it stood for over a century.

narrative_blocks.header: Married James Pierpont the year her father died
narrative_blocks.body: Her father, the Farmington minister Samuel Hooker, died in November 1697; she married James Pierpont of New Haven the following year. The match carried her from one parsonage to the center of Connecticut’s emerging intellectual capital.

narrative_blocks.header: Her daughter’s courtship under the elms
narrative_blocks.body: It was Mary’s daughter Sarah whom Jonathan Edwards courted beneath the parsonage elms in 1726 — a match made in her widowed household that joined the Pierpont and Edwards lines and led on to college presidents and a vice president.

narrative_blocks.header: A son lost crossing the Connecticut
narrative_blocks.body: Her son Samuel — Yale 1718, newly ordained at Lyme — drowned in March 1723 crossing the Connecticut River in a canoe with an Indian guide. His body came ashore on Fishers Island six weeks later and was buried where it was found.

narrative_blocks.header: The family that named an artist
narrative_blocks.body: The unidentified colonial painter who took her portrait around 1711 is known to art history only as the Pierpont Limner — christened for this family, whose likenesses anchor his surviving work. Her husband’s companion portrait hangs at the Yale University Art Gallery.

narrative_blocks.header: A silk apron may trace to her line
narrative_blocks.body: A rare 1730s embroidered silk apron at the Connecticut Historical Society descends through three generations of Marys beginning with her. Though scholars doubt she stitched it herself, her advantageous marriage made such a luxury possible for the daughters who followed.

bio.photo_notes: Portrait of Mrs. James Pierpont (née Mary Hooker), 1711. Wikipedia Commons.
